_meta.note: BuzzFeed-style book quiz · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked · js_001 kept verbatim (library)

title_001: The Great Gatsby Dopamine Master Quiz · F. Scott Fitzgerald
html_001: F. Scott Fitzgerald · 1925 · A self-portrait of Jazz Age America
html_002: A story of an impossible dream, a hand reaching for the green light
html_004: “So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.”
html_005: — the last line of the novel The Great Gatsby
html_006: Master Gatsby’s dream and the novel’s characters, symbols, and themes with
html_008:
.
        They start easy and get steadily harder,
html_009: and any question you miss comes back until you get it right.
html_026: That’s the end of the free preview
html_039: The Great Gatsby at a glance
html_040: Thread the whole novel into one line —
html_042: — and you’ll never forget it. It all begins with
html_043: Gatsby’s dream reaching for the green light
html_046: 🌃 The American Dream in the Jazz Age
html_049: 💚 Gatsby’s dream — win back an old love with wealth
html_052: 🥂 Parties and reunion — old money vs new money
html_053: The wall between East Egg and West Egg
html_055: 🥊 The Plaza showdown · Myrtle’s death · Gatsby shot
html_058: ⚰️ A dream in ruins, an empty funeral
html_061: Table of contents at a glance
html_065: Focused review of what you missed
html_067: walks you through the symbols and themes of The Great Gatsby, chapter by chapter.
html_073: Master a book’s core through dopamine-fueled repeat quizzes
html_074: This app is secondary learning content that reorganizes the book’s key ideas for study. For the full reading experience, please turn to the original work.

js_004: Who is the American author who wrote The Great Gatsby?
js_009: The Great Gatsby is a novel F. Scott Fitzgerald published in 1925. It’s counted among the masterpieces of modern American literature, painting Jazz Age America.
js_011: Who is the narrator who tells this novel’s story?
js_016: The story comes through the eyes and voice of Nick Carraway. He moves from the Midwest to New York, becomes Gatsby’s neighbor, and watches it all unfold.
js_018: Who is the protagonist throwing parties at a West Egg mansion?
js_020: The protagonist is Jay Gatsby. He amasses vast wealth and throws dazzling parties — yet all of it springs from a dream aimed at a single person.
js_022: Which era is the setting of this novel?
js_024: The turmoil right after the Civil War
js_025: The 1930s at the height of the Depression
js_026: The years of the Second World War
js_027: The setting is the summer of 1922, America in the so-called Jazz Age — a time when giddy prosperity and the emptiness behind it flowed side by side.
js_029: What color is the light at the end of the dock that Gatsby gazes at every night?
js_034: The light is green. Shining at the end of the dock by Daisy’s house, it becomes the symbol of the dream Gatsby chases.
js_036: What is the name of the old love Gatsby longs to meet again?
js_041: The one Gatsby yearns for is Daisy Buchanan. All his wealth and every party existed to win her back.
js_043: Who is the wealthy, arrogant man married to Daisy?
js_044: Daisy’s husband is Tom Buchanan. Rich and born to an elite family, he’s haughty and violent — and secretly seeing another woman.
js_046: Which two towns face each other across the bay as the novel’s twin stages?
js_048: Two cities, one Northern and one Southern
js_049: The two cities of Paris and New York
js_050: An upstream town and a downstream town
js_051: The two towns are East Egg and West Egg. Split by the bay, their contrast maps out the novel’s class structure.
js_053: What is Gatsby famous all over town for throwing every summer?
js_055: Secret political rallies held in private
js_058: Gatsby throws enormous parties. Crowds pour in uninvited, yet almost none of them actually know Gatsby himself.
js_060: In which year did The Great Gatsby first appear?
js_065: This novel was published in 1925. It didn’t sell much in the author’s lifetime, but was later reappraised as a classic representing America.
js_072: F. Scott Fitzgerald (1896–1940) was an American writer who defined the Jazz Age. Living through both dazzling success and downfall, he captured the light and shadow of his time better than anyone.
js_073: Nick Carraway is the narrator, a Midwesterner. More observer than participant, he watches and holds off on judgment — becoming the window through which readers see Gatsby.
js_074: Jay Gatsby went from penniless to fabulously rich. All his glamour is stage-setting for one dream: to win back the one person, Daisy.
js_075: The setting is the summer of 1922, an age of prosperity where Prohibition and jazz flowed together. Behind the giddy wealth, a moral emptiness was quietly growing.
js_076: The green light shines at the end of the dock by Daisy’s house. Gatsby reaches for it in the dark, and it recurs throughout the novel as the symbol of the dream he chases.
js_077: Daisy Buchanan is Gatsby’s old love. Beautiful but without the courage to leave her comfortable wealth, she ultimately betrays Gatsby’s dream.
js_078: Tom Buchanan is Daisy’s husband and a rich man of elite stock. Arrogant, violent, and keeping a mistress, he embodies the hypocrisy of old money.
js_079: East Egg and West Egg are two towns split by the bay. They stand for old money and new money, and the wall between them drives the novel’s conflict.
js_080: Even the uninvited crowd into Gatsby’s parties. Yet few actually know the host, revealing the loneliness inside all the glamour.
js_081: The Great Gatsby came out in 1925. The response was lukewarm in the author’s lifetime, but after his death it was reappraised as a classic of American literature.
js_094: The green light glows at the end of the dock by Daisy’s house. Gatsby reaching for it in the dark is the symbol of an unreachable dream — and of the American Dream itself.
js_095: The man who reinvented himself from penniless James Gatz into the tycoon Gatsby. Behind the glamour lies a single dream: to win back his old love, Daisy.
js_097: Two enormous eyes painted on a billboard in the Valley of Ashes. They read like the gaze of a god silently looking down on a world where morality has vanished — a striking symbol.
js_098: A gray wasteland between West Egg and New York. It symbolizes the people discarded in the shadow of prosperity — the dark underside of the American Dream.
js_099: Two towns split by the bay. East Egg stands for inherited old money, West Egg for the newly rich — drawing an insurmountable wall of class.
js_101: Daisy, who lacks the courage to leave her comfortable wealth, and Tom, arrogant and violent. Careless rich people who cause harm and then retreat, unruffled, into their own world.
js_103: The roaring 1920s. Gatsby’s wealth is hinted to come from illegal ventures like bootlegging under Prohibition, showing the light and shadow of the era at once.
js_105: A writer who lived through the Jazz Age’s glamour and its collapse. Watching his own era more sharply than anyone, he poured the illusion of the American Dream into this single book.
js_107: Nick’s view, and the green light
js_108: Nick, from the Midwest, moves to West Egg to learn the bond business.
js_109: Right next door is Gatsby’s mansion, where parties light up every night.
js_110: He’s invited to dinner at the home of his cousin Daisy and her husband, Tom Buchanan.
js_111: That night he sees Gatsby reaching through the dark toward the green light.
js_113: The Valley of Ashes and Gatsby’s party
js_114: The gray Valley of Ashes on the way to New York appears, along with Eckleburg’s eyes.
js_115: Tom introduces his mistress Myrtle Wilson to Nick, exposing his double life.
js_116: Nick is finally invited to one of Gatsby’s grand parties and meets him face to face.
js_117: Guests trade all kinds of rumors about Gatsby, but no one knows the truth.
js_120: Gatsby’s invented glamorous past and his long love for Daisy come to light.
js_121: Through Jordan, Gatsby asks Nick to arrange a reunion with Daisy.
js_122: Reunited awkwardly at Nick’s house, the two rekindle their old feelings.
js_123: Showing off his mansion, Gatsby feels the moment he dreamed of for five years has come true.
js_126: Tom grows suspicious of where Gatsby’s money comes from, and the two men grow tense.
js_127: On a sweltering day at the Plaza Hotel, Tom and Gatsby clash head-on over Daisy.
js_128: Daisy can’t bring herself to say she never once loved Tom.
js_129: On the way home, Gatsby’s car — with Daisy driving — hits and kills Myrtle.
js_132: Gatsby shields Daisy, ready to take the blame for the accident himself.
js_133: George Wilson, having lost his wife, misjudges Gatsby, shoots him, and then takes his own life.
js_134: Of all those party guests, almost no one comes to the funeral.
js_135: Disillusioned, Nick leaves the East and returns to his hometown.
js_137: The meaning of the green light
js_138: The green light becomes the symbol of an untouchable dream — the American Dream itself.
js_139: Gatsby’s dream — believing the past could be undone — finally runs aground.
js_140: The novel coolly reflects the moral emptiness lurking behind all that dazzling wealth.
js_141: So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.
js_142: Crowds pour into Gatsby’s mansion uninvited and revel all night. Yet almost no one truly knows the host, and almost no one comes to him in sincerity.
js_143: At the very peak of glamour the loneliness only deepens — making us ask again whether wealth and popularity can ever stand in for real connection.
js_144: The showdown at the Plaza Hotel
js_145: On a sweltering day, Tom and Gatsby collide head-on over Daisy. Gatsby wants her to say she never once loved Tom — but in the end she can’t.
js_146: The moment Gatsby’s dream of undoing the whole past shatters against reality, we see both the greatness and the recklessness of that dream at once.
js_148: Gatsby, who drew hundreds while alive, has almost no one at his funeral. Even Daisy retreats into her own world with Tom, without a single word.
js_149: It lays bare how hollow all those glittering connections were, exposing the irresponsibility and coldness of the world Gatsby chased.
js_150: Can the past really be undone? Is Gatsby’s belief great, or futile?
js_151: Think of Gatsby, who believed the past could be repeated. That obsession is what makes him both great and tragic.
js_152: If you gain wealth and success, is the American Dream complete?
js_153: Think about what Gatsby, who had everything, never managed to get.
js_154: Tom and Daisy caused harm and then calmly walked away — how should we see their irresponsibility?
js_155: Look at how wealth makes people irresponsible — you’ll find it in how they behave after Gatsby’s death.
js_159: You caught almost all the essentials
js_161: You’ve got the skeleton — one more round!
js_162: 🌫️ A passerby in the Valley of Ashes
js_163: The more you repeat, the higher your score

_app.title: The Great Gatsby Dopamine Master Quiz
_app.desc: An impossible dream, a hand reaching for the green light — master the classic in 30 quizzes
